title: Facilitated diffusion
description: Test your knowledge of facilitated diffusion!

assessment_items.question_content:
**If ATP hydrolysis is inhibited, which of the following types of movement across a cell membrane would likely also be inhibited?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Movement of an ion against its concentration gradient
Passage of water through aquaporins
Facilitated diffusion of a substance through channel proteins
Movement of carbon dioxide molecules down their concentration gradient

assessment_items.question_content:
**If ATP hydrolysis is inhibited, which of the following types of movement across a cell membrane would likely also be inhibited?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Movement of a substance from an area of lower concentration to an area of higher concentration
Movement of water through aquaporins
Passage of glucose across a membrane by passive transport.
Facilitated diffusion of ions through membrane channel proteins

assessment_items.question_content:
**If ATP hydrolysis is inhibited, which of the following types of movement across a cell membrane would likely also be inhibited?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Movement of a charged substance by active transport
Passage of water molecules through aquaporins
Diffusion of oxygen directly through the phospholipid bilayer
Movement of fructose from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration.

assessment_items.question_content:
The sarcolemma, or muscle cell plasma membrane, helps maintain a concentration gradient of calcium ions ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$) between a muscle cell’s cytoplasm and the extracellular fluid. When a muscle cell receives a signal to contract, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ moves down this gradient, passing from the extracellular space into the cytoplasm of the cell.

**Which of the following is the most likely mechanism by which $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ moves across the sarcolemma during muscle cell contraction?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ passes through membrane channel proteins.
$\text{Ca}^{2+}$ flows through aquaporins.
$\text{Ca}^{2+}$ is transported through transmembrane ATPases.
$\text{Ca}^{2+}$ directly diffuses through the membrane bilayer.

assessment_items.question_content:
An action potential occurs when a neuron sends an electrical signal down its axon. When at rest, the neuron’s plasma membrane helps maintain a concentration gradient of sodium ions ($\text{Na}^+$) between the neuron’s cytoplasm and the extracellular fluid. During an action potential, $\text{Na}^+$ moves down this gradient, passing from the extracellular space into the cytoplasm of the neuron.

**Which of the following is the most likely mechanism by which $\text{Na}^+$ moves across the plasma membrane of a neuron during an action potential?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: $\text{Na}^+$ passes through membrane channel proteins.
$\text{Na}^+$ flows through aquaporins.
$\text{Na}^+$ is transported through transmembrane ATPases.
$\text{Na}^+$ directly diffuses through the membrane bilayer.

assessment_items.question_content:
Gastric acid is a digestive fluid that is made with the help of parietal cells in the stomach lining. The parietal cell plasma membrane maintains a concentration gradient of potassium ions ($\text{K}^+$) between the cell cytoplasm and the extracellular fluid. During the formation of gastric acid, $\text{K}^+$ moves down this gradient, passing from the cytoplasm into the extracellular space.

**Which of the following is the most likely mechanism by which $\text{K}^+$ moves across the plasma membrane of a parietal cell during gastric acid formation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: $\text{K}^+$ passes through membrane channel proteins.
$\text{K}^+$ flows through aquaporins.
$\text{K}^+$ is transported through transmembrane ATPases.
$\text{K}^+$ directly diffuses through the membrane bilayer.

assessment_items.question_content:
The $\text{Na}^+$/$\text{ K}^+$ ATPase, or sodium-potassium pump, is a membrane protein that uses energy from ATP hydrolysis to transport sodium ions ($\text{Na}^+$) and potassium ions ($\text{K}^+$) against their concentration gradients. The steps of ion exchange carried out by the $\text{Na}^+$/$\text{ K}^+$ ATPase are depicted in the following diagram.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Given the concentration gradient established by the Na$^+$/K$^+$ ATPase, which of the following movements can occur?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: There are 4 boxes with images inside the boxes. The boxes are labeled from left to right 1, 2, 3, 4. All of the boxes show an image of a phospholipid bilayer with a membrane protein embedded in the phospholipid bilayer. The space above the bilayer on each image is labeled outside cell and there are 6 sodium ions and one potassium ion below label. The space below the bilayer on each image is labeled inside cell and there are 4 potassium ions and one sodium ion. Box one has a sodium ion on the inside of the cell with an arrow pointing into the open end of the membrane protein. Inside the membrane protein are two additional sodium ions, and the membrane protein is closed at the outside of the cell. Box two has an ATP attached to the closed portion of the membrane protein on the inside of the cell, and the membrane protein on the outside of the cell is open and has an arrow pointing from it towards 3 sodium ions that are in the outside of the cell.  Box 3 has an open membrane protein on the outside of the cell and there is a potassium ion on the outside of the cell with an arrow pointing towards the membrane protein. There is a second potassium ion within the membrane protein. On the inside of the cell the membrane protein is closed and there is a single phosphate attached to the closed membrane protein and then an ADP molecule on the inside of the cell. Box 4 has a closed membrane protein on the outside of the cell and an open membrane protein on the inside of the cell. There is an arrow pointing from the membrane protein towards two potassium ions inside the cell and there is also a phosphate on the inside of the cell.

choices.content: $\text{Na}^+$ can move into the cell by passive transport.
$\text{Na}^+$ can move out of the cell without using energy from ATP hydrolysis.
$\text{K}^+$ can move into the cell by passive transport.
$\text{K}^+$ can move out of the cell by directly diffusing through the cell membrane.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mitochondrial complex I is a large protein complex located in the inner mitochondrial membrane. During cellular respiration, this complex uses energy to transport hydrogen ions ($\text{H}^+$) against their concentration gradient. The steps of $\text{H}^+$ transport carried out by mitochondrial complex I are depicted in the following diagram.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Given the concentration gradient established by mitochondrial complex I, which of the following movements can occur?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: There are 2 images shown in 2 boxes side by side. Both images show a protein complex embedded in the phospholipid bilayer with 4 channels in the protein complex. The inner side of the phospholipid bilayer is labeled intermembrane space and the outside of the phospholipid bilayer is labeled mitochondrial matrix. The box on the left is labeled one and shows 3 hydrogen ions in the mitochondrial matrix and an additional 4 hydrogen ions at the top of the 4 channels in the protein complex within the mitochondrial matrix. There are arrows points from each hydrogen ion through a protein channel and into the intermembrane space. In the intermembrane space are 6 hydrogen ions. The box on the right is labeled two and shows 3 hydrogen ions in the mitochondrial matrix and arrows in each of the 4 protein complex channels that point to 4 hydrogen ions located in the intermembrane space. The intermembrane space also has an additional 6 hydrogen ions located in it.

choices.content: $\text{H}^+$ can move into the mitochondrial matrix without a direct input of energy.
$\text{H}^+$ can move into the intermembrane space by passive transport.
$\text{H}^+$ can move into the mitochondrial matrix by directly diffusing through the inner mitochondrial membrane.
$\text{H}^+$ can move into the intermembrane space without the help of a membrane protein.

assessment_items.question_content:
The gastric $\text{H}^+$/$\text{ K}^+$ ATPase is a membrane protein that uses energy from ATP hydrolysis to transport hydrogen ions ($\text{H}^+$) and potassium ions ($\text{K}^+$) against their concentration gradients. The steps of ion exchange carried out by the $\text{H}^+$/$\text{ K}^+$ ATPase are depicted in the following diagram.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Given the concentration gradient established by the $\text{H}^+$/$\text{K}^+$ ATPase, which of the following movements can occur?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: There are 2 images shown in boxes side by side. Both images show a phospholipid bilayer with a channel protein embedded in the bilayer. Above the phospholipid bilayers there are 5 hydrogen ions and one potassium ion under the label outside cell. Below the phospholipid bilayers there are 6 potassium ions and one hydrogen ion above the label inside cell. The box on the left is labeled one and it shows a potassium ion on the outside of the cell with an arrow pointing into channel protein. On the inside of the cell there is a hydrogen ion with an arrow pointing into the channel protein. Attached to the protein channel on the inside of the cell is ATP. The box on the right is labeled two and it shows an arrow coming out of the channel protein towards the outside of the cell and the arrow is pointing at a hydrogen ion. There is also an arrow inside the protein channel pointing towards the inside of the cell and the arrow is pointing at a potassium ion. Attached to the protein channel on the inside of the cell is ADP.

choices.content: $\text{K}^+$ can move out of the cell without using energy from ATP hydrolysis.
$\text{K}^+$ can move into the cell by passive transport.
$\text{H}^+$ can move out of the cell by passive transport.
$\text{H}^+$ can move into the cell by directly diffusing through the cell membrane.

assessment_items.question_content:
The movement of ions across a cell membrane can generate a membrane potential, or a voltage difference, across the membrane.

In neurons, this occurs when sodium-potassium pumps, or $\text{Na}^+$/$\text{ K}^+$ ATPases, transport sodium ions ($\text{Na}^+$) and potassium ions ($\text{K}^+$) across the cell membrane against their concentration gradients. The action of these pumps, combined with the diffusion of certain ions through protein channels in the membrane, results in a resting membrane potential of approximately $-70\,\text{mV}$ with respect to the inside of the cell.

**Which of the following diagrams best represents the resting membrane potential of a neuron?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

choices.content: ![The image shows a vertically oriented phospholipid bilayer with one side labeled outside of neuron and the other side labeled inside of neuron. On the outside of the neuron there are 4 positive symbols arranged vertically and on the inside of the neuron there are 4 negative symbols arranged vertically.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5f32ac53eab8523a8f2eb12a745567bd.png)

![The image shows a vertically oriented phospholipid bilayer with one side labeled outside of neuron and the other side labeled inside of neuron. On the outside of the neuron from top to bottom there is a positive symbol, a negative symbol, a positive symbol and a negative symbol.  On the inside of the neuron from top to bottom there is a negative symbol, a positive symbol, a negative symbol, and a positive symbol.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/39c66f2f914fd221b2416cf5e0da30b6.png)

![The image shows a vertically oriented phospholipid bilayer with one side labeled outside of neuron and the other side labeled inside of neuron. There are 4 positive symbols arranged vertically on both the outside of the neuron and the inside of the neuron.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/64db059fbe942708a30dae83e0d73b22.png)

![The image shows a vertically oriented phospholipid bilayer with one side labeled outside of neuron and the other side labeled inside of neuron. There are 4 negative symbols arranged vertically on both the outside of the neuron and the inside of the neuron. ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/deee918a4022aa763258bb4e17f8a6c5.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
Neurons transmit information in the form of electrical signals called action potentials. When at rest, a neuron has a membrane potential of approximately $-70\,\text{mV}$ with respect to the inside of the cell. However, during an action potential, an influx of positively charged ions causes the membrane to depolarize. Immediately following depolarization, the membrane potential of the neuron is approximately $+30\,\text{mV}$ with respect to the inside of the cell.

**Which of the following diagrams best represents the membrane potential of a neuron immediately after depolarization?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

choices.content: ![The image shows a vertically oriented phospholipid bilayer with one side labeled outside of neuron and the other side labeled inside of neuron. On the outside of the neuron there are 4 negative symbols arranged vertically and on the inside of the neuron there are 4 positive symbols arranged vertically.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3c9cfd09694f926f2731ac361d7086ee.png)

![The image shows a vertically oriented phospholipid bilayer with one side labeled outside of neuron and the other side labeled inside of neuron. On the inside of the neuron from top to bottom there is a positive symbol, a negative symbol, a positive symbol and a negative symbol.  On the outside of the neuron from top to bottom there is a negative symbol, a positive symbol, a negative symbol, and a positive symbol.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/878c7bd8dc3f80ae6c0a3ef4ca6d9a14.png)

![The image shows a vertically oriented phospholipid bilayer with one side labeled outside of neuron and the other side labeled inside of neuron. There are 4 positive symbols arranged vertically on both the outside of the neuron and the inside of the neuron.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/00a9f4c65590d8ad65870ff0ca3c985e.png)

![The image shows a vertically oriented phospholipid bilayer with one side labeled outside of neuron and the other side labeled inside of neuron. There are 4 negative symbols arranged vertically on both the outside of the neuron and the inside of the neuron.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/18f6b81654951559833a4fa50bac4d72.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
Neurons transmit information in the form of electrical signals called action potentials. During an action potential, an influx of positively charged ions causes the membrane potential to change from its resting value of approximately $-70\,\text{mV}$ to approximately $+30\,\text{mV}$. This is immediately followed by the movement of more positively charged ions in the opposite direction, which helps return the membrane potential to its resting value. This process is represented in the following graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following statements best describes how the inside and the outside of the neuron are charged at Point A in the graph above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The x axis of the line graph is labeled time measured in milliseconds. The marked intervals on the x axis are 0,1,2,3,4, and 5. The y axis is labeled membrane potential measured in millivolts. The marks on the y axis are negative 70, 0 and positive 30. The line of the graph begins at negative 70 on the y axis and remains horizontal on the x axis until 1 millisecond. At the middle of this segment of the line is a red dot labeled A. At 1 millisecond the line begins to ascend sharply and peaks at 2, positive 30. The line then begins to rapidly descend and reaches a curved point between 3 and 4 milliseconds and below the negative 70 on the y axis. From this low point, the line then begins to ascend between 4 and 5 milliseconds and at 5 milliseconds the line flattens out at negative 70 on the y axis.
options.caption: **Figure 1.** The change in membrane potential during an action potential in a neuron. The membrane potential is measured with respect to the inside of the neuron.

choices.content: The inside of the neuron has a net negative charge, and the outside has a net positive charge.
Neither the inside nor the outside of the neuron has a net charge.
Both the inside and outside of the neuron have a net positive charge.
Both the inside and outside of the neuron have a net negative charge.
